coredata.dc:title: Selective detection of rutin at novel pyridinic-nitrogen-rich carbon dots derived from chicken feet biowaste: The role of bovine serum albumin during the assay
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Alkahtani, Saad A.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Alqahtani, Yahya S.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: A simple fluorescence method is described for measuring rutin dependent on the nitogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) prepared via simple pyrolysis method from chicken feet biowaste. The as-fabricated NCDs have unique advantages including cost-effectiveness and high quantum yield (42.9 %). The as-prepared NCDs explore an optimal emission band at 430 nm following exciting NCDs at 330 nm. Addition of rutin to blue-emissive NCDs quenched their fluorescence emission by inner-filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence responses increased as the rutin amount was raised from 100 to 1000 nmol/L with 5.3 nmol/L as a detection limit (S/N = 3). The probe selectivity was improved by adding bovine serum albumin (BSA), which binds other structurally-related compounds (other flavonoids). The as-synthesized NCDs exhibited some advantages towards rutin detection such as: lower LOD value, satisfactorily reproducibility, simplicity, rapidity, selectivity, and stability. The sensing probe was efficiently utilized for determining rutin in different real samples with acceptable results. The sensor offers an efficient biowaste-based approach for the determination of (bio) molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rutin
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Bovine serum albumin
Flavonoids
Chicken feet biowaste
Real samples

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Materials/ reagents, instrumentation, and quantum yield calculation
Steps for fluorometric determination of rutin

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/1).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern; (B) TEM (inset is HR-TEM): (C) particle distribution: (D) FT-IR of NCDs.
(A) Absorption and fluorescence spectra of NCDs while (B) is excitation-dependent emission spectra.
Fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs with increasing amounts of rutin in the range of 100–1000 nmol/L. Inset represents a calibration plot between F°/F and concentration of rutin (nmol/L).
Selectivity of NCDs towards detection of 100 nmol/L in the presence of 250 µmol/L interfering species; except, in the presence of flavonoids 15.0 µL of 50.0 µmol/L BSA were added.
Preparation of NCDs and its utility for determination of rutin.
Summary of the optimum experimental variables for determination of rutin.
Comparison between various fluorometric sensors for assay of rutin.
Detection of rutin in pharmaceutical tablets, human serum, and lemon juice using NCDs (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Selective detection of rutin at novel pyridinic-nitrogen-rich carbon dots derived from chicken feet biowaste: The role of bovine serum albumin during the assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple fluorescence method is described for measuring rutin dependent on the nitogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) prepared via simple pyrolysis method from chicken feet biowaste. The as-fabricated NCDs have unique advantages including cost-effectiveness and high quantum yield (42.9 %). The as-prepared NCDs explore an optimal emission band at 430 nm following exciting NCDs at 330 nm. Addition of rutin to blue-emissive NCDs quenched their fluorescence emission by inner-filtration effect (IFE) and static quenching. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescence responses increased as the rutin amount was raised from 100 to 1000 nmol/L with 5.3 nmol/L as a detection limit (S/N = 3). The probe selectivity was improved by adding bovine serum albumin (BSA), which binds other structurally-related compounds (other flavonoids). The as-synthesized NCDs exhibited some advantages towards rutin detection such as: lower LOD value, satisfactorily reproducibility, simplicity, rapidity, selectivity, and stability. The sensing probe was efficiently utilized for determining rutin in different real samples with acceptable results. The sensor offers an efficient biowaste-based approach for the determination of (bio) molecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rutin is a flavonoid glycoside that occurs naturally in a variety of plants, including wolfberry, buckwheat, flavedo, and red date [1,2]. It possesses many medical applications including anti-hypertension, antioxidant, anti-allergic, antitumor, and others. Moreover, it is used for treating hemorrhagic purpura, retinal hemorrhage, acute hemorrhagic nephritis, and postmortem hemorrhage [3,4]. Consuming too much rutin can have a negative impact on the body by affecting the role of oxidoreductase, which can in turn impact the physiological activities of the thyroid gland. This can prevent the proper oxidation of adrenaline, leading to potential health issues [5,6]. Accurately measuring the amount of rutin present in clinical samples, tablets, and food products is crucial in order to properly adjust the oral dosage of rutin and enhance its bioavailability. Rutin was analyzed qualitatively and quantitatively using different techniques such as HPLC/UV [7], CE/electrochemical detection [8], colorimetry [9], and electrochemical [10] techniques. Out of the various methods available, utilizing fluorometry has several advantages such as its ability to provide a rapid response, ease of visualization, high sensitivity, and overall simplicity [11]. In addition, it has the capability to determine (bio) molecules in real time through low-cost strategy using tailored-made photoluminscent sensors [12].

In the last decade, carbon dots (CDs) have gained great attentions as a result of photo-bleaching resistance, low-toxicity, adequate photoluminescence property, chemical versatility, water solubility, and ease-of-surface functionalization [13–15]. These fascinating properties make CDs ideal candidates for fluorescent sensing of various analytes. The photoluminescence characteristics of certain materials can be improved by incorporating heteroatoms into their structure. This is because the heteroatoms facilitate the transfer of charges between the carbon atoms, which are electron-deficient, and the heteroatoms, which are electron-rich [16–20]. One of these heteroatoms is nitrogen (N) which tends to passivate the surface of CDs, enhancing their photoluminescence intensity. Poultry by-products e.g. chicken feet contain high amounts of protein such as alanine, glycine, proline, and glutamic acid [21,22]. Since biomass waste has large amount of protein and has low environmental impact, it is commonly used as a starting material for the production of nitrogen-rich-CDs, which contributes to the significant fluorescence intensity [23].

Herein, novel pyridinic-nitrogen-rich CDs (NCDs) were fabricated from chicken feet biowaste, and used for the “Turn-off” determination of rutin. The as-fabricated NCDs possessed unique properties including high photoluminescence quantum yield, good water solubility, and excellent stability, making it an ideal sensor for rutin quantitation in real specimens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials/ reagents, instrumentation, and quantum yield calculation
ce:section.ce:para: See Supporting Information.

Pyrolysis method was used to fabricate NCDs where 100 g of chicken feet were taken and pyrolyzed at 300 °C for 5 h. After that, the resultant product was crushed and sieved with 1 mm pore size. Then, collect the powder and disperse it in deionized water (DI) before dialysis (MWCO = 3 KDa). Ultimately, the dialyzed solution was lyophilized to get a solid product, (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steps for fluorometric determination of rutin
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared NCDs (0.5 mL, 0.1 mg mL−1) was added to 1.0 mL phosphate buffered solution of pH 7.0 and various rutin aliquots before completing the solution to 5.0 mL by DI. Then, incubate the solution for one min at room temperature (RT). Finally, the spectra were collected at 430 nm following exciting the solution at 330 nm.

Twenty tablets were carefully ground and the average of one tablet was suspended in 20 mL DI with the aid of sonication for 20 min. Then, complete the resultant suspension to 50 mL with DI before filtration using medium filter paper. The analytical procedures were applied before and after spiking with different amounts of rutin.

Human plasma (1.0 mL) was blended with certain amount of rutin, followed by 1.25 mL acetonitrile. Then, centrifuge the mixture for 30 min at 3500 rpm. The clear supernatant was withdrawn and refrigerated at −20 °C until usage.

30.0 g of the edible part was chopped into small parts, followed by immersion in 50 mL ethanol. After that, the supernatant was filtered using medium filter paper, and the resultant solution was diluted five times by phosphate buffered solution of pH 7.0.

The XRD pattern of NCDs was investigated in Fig. 1 A was a diffraction peak centered at 23.8°, corresponding to (002) graphite diffraction plane (d002) = 3.34 A° [24]. The increased interlayer spacing observed in the as-prepared NCDs, relative to graphitic carbon, is attributed to existence of oxygen and nitrogen atoms incorporated within the structure of NCDs [25]. Fig. 1B explores the TEM image of the as-synthesized NCDs with uniformly distributed nanoparticles and average diameter of 5.2 nm (Fig. 1C). The inset of Fig. 1B represents the high resolution TEM of NCDs with the lattice fringes of the NCDs which are clearly separated and spaced at 0.34 nm, which is thought to correspond to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. The findings of HR-TEM agreed well with XRD data, suggesting the graphitic structure of the as-fabricated NCDs. The FT-IR scan of the NCDs displays a broad band located at 3420 cm−1, assigning to OH and NH2 groups. The stretching vibrations of CH2 are associated with small FT-IR bands located at 2815 and 2920 cm−1. The stretching vibration of C=O/C=N groups is associated with the band located at 1690 cm−1, whereas the stretching vibration of C=C is assigned to the band at 1480 cm−1 [26,27]. The stretching vibrations of C–O/C–N and C–C are respectively attributed to the bands located at 1160 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1. The sharp band at 755 cm−1 corresponds to the out-of-plane bending of N–H [28], Fig. 1D. The XPS survey revealed that the as-fabricated NCDs have typical signals of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s with binding energies of 286 eV, 397 eV, and 529 eV, respectively (Fig. S1A). Fig. S1B represents the C 1s spectrum with distinctive peaks at 282.4 eV, 283.2 eV, and 284.5 eV, which correspond to C=C/C–C, C–N, and C = O/C = N, respectively [29]. Fig. S1C exhibits the high resolution XPS of N 1s with sharp peaks at 397.4 eV and 398.7 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyridinic N and amidic/amino N, respectively [30]. Fig. S1D displays the high resolution XPS of O 1s with distinctive peaks at 528.6 eV and 529.2 eV, respectively, assigned to C=O and C–O–C/C–OH, respectively [31]. The elemental analysis data of XPS scans were tabulated in Table S1. The results in the table confirmed that the as-prepared NCDs consist mainly of pyridinic-nitrogen (84.34 % of N 1s). Fig. S2 exhibits the Raman spectrum of the produced NCDs with two peaks at 1352 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1, assigning to the G- and D-bands of graphite-like structure. The IG/ID ratio of NCDs was found to be 1.08, suggesting the graphitic nature of NCDs [32,33].

To examine the optical properties of the NCDs that were prepared, spectroscopic techniques such as UV/Vis spectrophotometry and spectrofluorometry were utilized (Fig. 2 ). It is clearly seen that the NCDs exhibits two absorption bands at 227 nm and 280 nm, which are assigned to π-π* of C=C and n-π* of C=O/C=N transitions, respectively. Moreover, excitation and emission spectra of the as-synthesized NCDs were recorded in Fig. 2A with noticeable color change in the day light and after UV exposure. Upon excitation at 330 nm, the sensing interface displayed a noticeably intense blue emission at 430 nm. During excitation, the NCDs’ surface functional groups had a significant influence on NCDs’ optical properties as they control the trapping of excitons on the surface, leading to the process of “radiative-recombination” [34]. In addition, the fluorescence wavelengths and intensities were monitored as a function of excitation wavelength (Fig. 2B). Experimental results showed that there was a shift towards longer wavelengths in the emission bands, from 370 nm to 490 nm, if the wavelength of excitation was shifted from 300 nm toward 400 nm. This observation suggests that the emission behavior is influenced by the excitation wavelength. The reason behind this behavior can be attributed to distribution of numerous emitting centers on NCDs’ surface. These emissive centers are formed because of existence of diverse functional groups with varying energy levels [35]. The red-shift occurred with increasing the excitation wavelength enables NCDs to be unique in different applications as emission wavelength could be tailored by variation of excitation wavelength. The fluorescence quantum yield of the as-prepared NCDs was calculated against quinine sulfate and was found to be 42.9 %, which could be attributed to the presence of nitrogen, OH, and CONH– groups on the surface of NCDs. The inclusion of nitrogen in the sample enhances the spin–orbit coupling for the emission process of intersystem crossing, and significantly modifies the overall electronic structure of the sample. The intrinsic state emission is facilitated by the existence of functional groups like OH, NH2, and amide, which also inhibit non-radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. As a result, fluorescence emission is produced and there is a marked increase in the quantum yield of the NCDs.

Stability of the as-prepared NCDs was investigated in Fig. S3. In Fig. S3A, it can be observed that subjecting the NCDs to 240 min of UV illumination did not significantly impact the fluorescence emission. Additionally, exposing the NCDs to high salt concentrations did not have any significant effect on the emission intensity, as demonstrated in Fig. S3B. The emission characteristics of the NCDs were also analyzed at varying pH levels (Fig. S3C). It is obvious that in high acidic pH values, the fluorescence emissions were greatly reduced, which may be attributed to the aggregation of NCDs, favoring the non-radiative recombination. In high alkaline, the fluorescence readings were also reduced as a result of deprotonation of “COOH” and dissociation of “CONH”. Fig. S3D illustrates that NCDs were subjected to varying concentrations of H2O2 (ranging from 0.1 mM to 10 mM) to assess their resistance to oxidation. The results indicate that NCDs were stable across the range of H2O2 concentrations tested. Similarly, in Fig. S3E, the impact of different solvents on the fluorescence of NCDs was investigated, and it was observed that NCDs displayed the same fluorescence intensity across all solvents examined.

For improving the fluorescence sensitivity of NCDs sensor to determine rutin, the experimental conditions were investigated. The conditions for detecting rutin with NCDs were optimized by adjusting the pH, reaction time, amount of NCDs, temperature, and excitation wavelength, and measuring the emission intensity change at 430 nm. The impact of varying excitation wavelengths was previously addressed. However, the effects of modifying other experimental conditions were demonstrated in Fig. S4, and the optimal parameters were summarized in Table 1 .

The reproducibility of the as-fabricated NCDs was estimated via measuring the responses of 50 nmol/L after preparing six different probes fabricated under the same conditions. The results showed that the percentage of relative standard deviation (% RSD) did not surpass 3.8%, referring to the reproducibility of NCDs for measuring rutin. In addition, the stability of the probe was measured by recording the fluorometric response of -150 nmol/L for 24 days. It was found that the relative activity of the probe did not significantly change during this period, confirming the long-term stability of the as-fabricated NCDs.

Fig. 4 illustrates the investigation of how coexisting species affect the reduction of fluorescence emission in the as-fabricated NCDs. These interfering species include common ions, biological compounds, and structurally-related compounds (interfering flavonoids). It was found that about 250 µmol/L of coexisting species did not significantly change NCDs emission; only rutin quenched the NCDs emission obviously. It should be noted that the interference from other flavonoids could be eliminated by addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA). Based on the findings, bovine serum albumin (BSA) was shown to bind interfering flavonoids at a rate five times higher compared to rutin. This is likely due to the sugar moiety present in rutin, which decreases the binding affinity between rutin and the hydrophobic BSA. In other words, the sugar group on rutin reduces how tightly it binds to BSA [3,10]. Therefore, 15.0 µL of 50.0 µmol/L BSA were added during the determination of rutin in lemon juice using NCDs to prevent the interference of structurally-related flavonoids (Fig. S5). Fig. S5A& Fig. S5B explore the absorption spectra of rutin and quercetin (example of structurally-related compounds), respectively. It is obvious that in absence of BSA (Fig. S5A), they almost exhibit the same absorption spectra; but, in the presence of BSA the absorbance value of quercetin was significantly reduced, referring to the binding of BSA to subdomain IIA of BSA (Fig. S5B), [40]. Moreover, the fluorescence spectroscopy was used to monitor the interaction between BSA and quercetin (Fig. S5C&D). In absence of BSA, NCDs were quenched by rutin and quercetin at nearly the same level (Fig. S5C); while, the addition of BSA binds quercetin and prevents its quenching effect on the as-prepared NCDs (Fig. S5D). To the end, the addition of BSA prevents the interference of structurally-related compounds upon the determination of rutin in food products e.g. lemon-juice.

The as-synthesized NCDs were used to estimate the amount of rutin in some matrices such as pharmaceutical tablets, human serum, and lemon juice (Table 3 ). The samples were prepared and measured before and following treated with varied rutin amounts. The recoveries % values ranged from 97.5 % to 102.3 % and RSD % not more than 3.4 %, confirming the accuracy of the designed probe for measuring rutin. Moreover, the results were compared with a reported method and the results are agreed well, affirming the accuracy of the as-fabricated probe.

Several mechanisms of the fluorescence quenching are well-known including photo-induced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner-filtration effect (IFE). Stern-Volmer plot is used to distinguish between static and collisional (dynamic) quenching. Static quenching occurs due to the interaction between the fluorophore and quencher while they are in their ground state, resulting in forming a non-emissive coordinated complex. In the dynamic quenching, it is concerned with the formation of a complex between the quencher and excited fluorophore [41]. In order to determine the mechanism of the emission quenching, quenching experiments were conducted at three distinct temperatures (298 K, 308 K, and 318 K) (Fig. S6A). It was found that the slope (quenching constant, Ksv) increased with decreasing the temperature, referring to a static quenching process [41]. As seen in Fig. S6B, rutin absorption peak and NCDs emission/excitation spectra overlap confirming the existence of IFE between rutin and NCDs. The fluorescence lifetimes were recorded in the absence (τ = 6.23 ns) and presence (τ = 6.18 ns) of rutin (Fig. S6C). It was found that the values did not change after addition of rutin, confirming a static quenching between them. To the end, the quenching of NCDs by rutin may be a combination between IFE and static quenching.

To further verify the fluorescence quenching caused by IFE, the Parker Eqs. (1), (2) and (3) was applied.
(1)

cor

2.3 d

cor , o
Where Fobs represents the observed emission intensity, Fcor represents the correcting emission intensity following accounting for IFE.Aem andAex represent the light absorption at 430 and 330 nm, respectively and (g)-value is 0.40 cm and indicates the distance between the excitation beam's edge and the cuvette's edge. The excitation beam thickness (0.10 cm) is denoted by (s) while thelight pathlength in solution (1.0 cm) is denoted by (d). Fobs and Fobs,0 represent the observed fluorescence signals with and without rutin. Fcor and Fcor,0 represent the corrected fluorescence signals following removing IFE influence with and without rutin. The applied variables of the equations were computed and presented in Table S2. As demonstrated in Table S2 and Fig. S7, raising rutin amounts, increase Fcor/Fobs ratio and the suppressed efficiency of observed (Eobs), and the Eobs value was greater than the Ecor value.The previously mentioned research findings strongly suggest that IFE is the quenching mode.

In a conclusion, a blue fluorescent NCDs sensor was fabricated from chicken feet biowaste through simple pyrolysis method. The as-synthesized NCDs were applied to assay rutin in pharmaceutical tablets, human serum, and lemon juice samples with acceptable recoveries % ranged from 97.5 % to 102.3 % and RSD % value and RSD % not more than 3.4 %, affirming the accuracy of the proposed fluorometric method. Addition of rutin quenched the blue fluorescence of NCDs via inner-filtration effect and static quenching. The selectivity of the proposed probe was improved via addition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) that binds other structurally-related compounds stronger than rutin. Advantages of the as-fabricated sensor include simplicity, rapid-response, selectivity, wide-linear range, and sensitivity. The sensor offers a biowaste-based probe for the determination of (bio) molecules.

Saad A. Alkahtani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Yahya S. Alqahtani: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/DRP/MRC/12/1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123252.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
